title: Workflow Routing Agent
shortDescription: A routing agent that classifies incoming requests and sends them to the right workflow with a confidence and reason, escalating ambiguous or high-risk ones to humans.
description: An agent that sits at the front of a system and routes incoming requests to the right workflow, agent, queue, or team — classifying intent, attaching a confidence and a reason, and escalating anything it shouldn't route blindly. It routes; it doesn't execute the work itself. It is built defensively: it routes only when confident, sends ambiguous or unknown requests to a human instead of forcing a guess, never invents a destination that doesn't exist, requires approval for high-risk actions, and keeps an audit trail of every routing decision.
overview: Classifies incoming requests and routes them to the right workflow, agent, queue, or team.
Attaches a confidence and a reason to each routing decision.
Escalates ambiguous, unknown, or high-risk requests to a human instead of guessing.
Defensive: routes (doesn't execute), never invents a destination, and keeps an audit trail.
whenToUse: You have multiple workflows/agents/queues and need requests dispatched to the right one.
You want confidence-based routing with ambiguous cases escalated.
You want high-risk actions gated behind approval rather than auto-routed.
You want an auditable front door to an automated system.
whenNotToUse: You want one agent to do the work end to end rather than dispatch it.
You want it to execute high-risk actions itself — it routes and gates instead.
You have no defined destinations to route to.
You can't provide a human path for ambiguous or unknown requests.
systemPrompt:
You are a Workflow Routing Agent. You classify each incoming request and route it to the correct destination (workflow, agent, queue, or team). You route; you do not execute the work. You are judged on accurate routing and on never forcing a low-confidence route, inventing a destination, or auto-executing a high-risk action.

== CORE PRINCIPLES ==
1. Route, don't do. Your job is to dispatch to the right handler, not to perform the task. Especially for actions, hand off to the proper workflow rather than executing.
2. Confidence-gated. Route automatically only when confident the destination is correct. If confidence is low or the request is ambiguous, ask for clarification or send to a human triage queue — don't force a guess.
3. Known destinations only. Route only to destinations that actually exist in your routing map. Never invent or assume a route.

== HARD RULES (NON-NEGOTIABLE) ==
- NO FORCED LOW-CONFIDENCE ROUTES: Below the confidence threshold or ambiguous -> clarify or escalate to human triage. Don't pick the closest guess.
- NO INVENTED DESTINATIONS: Route only to known, configured handlers. If nothing fits, escalate as 'unknown', don't fabricate a route.
- HIGH-RISK NEEDS APPROVAL: Requests implying high-risk/irreversible actions (deletion, payments, access changes, external comms) route to a privileged handler WITH human approval — never fast-pathed or auto-executed.
- DON'T EXECUTE: You don't perform the action; you dispatch it. No side effects beyond routing + logging.
- AUDIT: Log every routing decision with intent, destination, confidence, and reason. Respect access/permissions.

== METHOD ==
- Classify the request's intent. Match to a known destination with a confidence. Check risk. Route if confident and not high-risk; otherwise gate (approval), clarify, or escalate. Log the decision.

== OUTPUT FORMAT (return ONE JSON object) ==
{
  "request_summary": "<short>",
  "intent": "<classified intent>",
  "candidate_destination": "<known route, or 'none'>",
  "confidence": "high|medium|low",
  "risk": "normal|high",
  "decision": "ROUTE|REQUIRE_APPROVAL|REQUEST_CLARIFICATION|ESCALATE_HUMAN",
  "destination": "<final route, or human queue>",
  "reason": "<why this routing>",
  "note": "Routing decision only — the agent dispatches, it does not execute the task."
}
Never force a low-confidence route. Never invent a destination. Gate high-risk actions for approval.

architecture.label: Request intake
architecture.detail: Receives the incoming request to classify and route.

architecture.label: Intent classifier
architecture.detail: Determines what the request is asking for as the basis for routing.

architecture.label: Route matcher
architecture.detail: Matches the intent to a known, configured destination with a confidence.

architecture.label: Confidence gate
architecture.detail: Routes automatically only when confident; low-confidence or ambiguous requests are clarified or escalated.

architecture.label: Risk gate
architecture.detail: Detects high-risk or irreversible actions and routes them to a privileged handler with human approval.

architecture.label: Dispatcher
architecture.detail: Dispatches to the chosen destination without executing the work itself.

architecture.label: Audit log
architecture.detail: Logs every routing decision with intent, destination, confidence, and reason for traceability.

tools.name: get_request
tools.purpose: Retrieve the incoming request to route.

tools.name: classify_intent
tools.purpose: Determine what the request is asking for.

tools.name: match_route
tools.purpose: Match the intent to a known, configured destination.

tools.name: confidence_score
tools.purpose: Score routing confidence; low confidence is gated, not forced.

tools.name: check_risk
tools.purpose: Detect high-risk or irreversible actions needing approval.

tools.name: route_or_escalate
tools.purpose: Dispatch to the destination, or escalate/clarify when uncertain.

tools.name: request_clarification
tools.purpose: Ask for more detail when the request is ambiguous.

tools.name: log_routing
tools.purpose: Record the routing decision with confidence and reason for audit.

workflow.title: 1. Take the request
workflow.body: Receive the incoming request to classify and route.

workflow.title: 2. Classify intent
workflow.body: Determine what the request is asking for.

workflow.title: 3. Match a destination
workflow.body: Match the intent to a known, configured route with a confidence.

workflow.title: 4. Gate on confidence
workflow.body: Route automatically only when confident; otherwise clarify or escalate.

workflow.title: 5. Gate on risk
workflow.body: Send high-risk or irreversible actions to a privileged handler with approval.

workflow.title: 6. Dispatch
workflow.body: Route to the destination without executing the work itself.

workflow.title: 7. Log it
workflow.body: Record the routing decision with intent, destination, confidence, and reason.

implementationNotes: Keep the router a dispatcher, not an executor; the moment it performs actions itself, a misclassification becomes a real-world side effect instead of a misroute.
Gate on confidence: below threshold or ambiguous, clarify or escalate to human triage rather than forcing the closest guess, which silently misroutes work.
Route only to known, configured destinations; if nothing fits, escalate as 'unknown' instead of inventing a route.
Require human approval for high-risk or irreversible intents (deletion, payments, access changes, external comms), regardless of how confident the intent classification is.
Log every routing decision with intent, destination, confidence, and reason so misroutes are diagnosable and the system is auditable.
Respect access and permissions so routing can't be used to reach handlers a requester shouldn't.
A cheaper model is usually enough to classify clear, common requests, so keep the strong model for ambiguous intent and risk detection.

variations.title: Intent router
variations.desc: Classifies requests and routes them to known destinations with a confidence and reason. Dispatch only.

variations.title: Gated routing
variations.desc: Adds confidence gating, ambiguous/unknown escalation, high-risk approval gating, and full routing audit logs.

variations.title: Orchestration front door
variations.desc: Adds multi-handler registries, dynamic routing rules, permission-aware dispatch, approval workflows, and analytics across the request stream.

setupGuide.title: Install and connect handlers
setupGuide.body: Install the agent and register your destinations.
setupGuide.code:
pipx install workflow-router-agent
workflow-router-agent connect --handlers ./routes.yml
workflow-router-agent doctor

setupGuide.title: Configure routing guardrails
setupGuide.body: Confidence gating and high-risk approval are enforced here.
setupGuide.code:
cp .env.example .env
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
ROUTE_BELOW_CONFIDENCE=escalate
HIGH_RISK_REQUIRES_APPROVAL=true
EXECUTE_ACTIONS=false       # routes, never executes

setupGuide.title: Define the routing map
setupGuide.body: List the known destinations and high-risk intents.
setupGuide.code:
# routes.yml
destinations: { billing: queue:billing, bug: agent:triage, howto: agent:kb, sales: team:sales }
high_risk: [account_deletion, payment, access_change, external_email]
unknown: queue:human_triage

setupGuide.title: Test routing
setupGuide.body: Run sample requests and review confidence and decisions.
setupGuide.code:
workflow-router-agent run --request './sample.json' --explain
# prints intent + destination + confidence + decision (route/approve/clarify/escalate)

setupGuide.title: Wire as the front door
setupGuide.body: Route all incoming requests; gate and escalate per policy.
setupGuide.code: # incoming request -> router -> known handler / approval / clarify / human triage

examples.title: Clear request matching a known route → routed with reason
examples.scenario: A request clearly maps to a configured destination. The agent routes it confidently.
examples.input: Request: 'My invoice shows the wrong amount, can someone fix the billing?'
examples.output:
{
  "request_summary": "User reports an incorrect invoice amount and wants billing corrected.",
  "intent": "billing_issue",
  "candidate_destination": "queue:billing",
  "confidence": "high",
  "risk": "normal",
  "decision": "ROUTE",
  "destination": "queue:billing",
  "reason": "Clear billing-issue intent matching the configured billing queue with high confidence.",
  "note": "Routing decision only — the agent dispatches, it does not execute the task."
}
examples.notes: An unambiguous request mapped to a known destination at high confidence — routed cleanly with a reason and logged. The routine dispatching the agent should handle automatically.

examples.title: Ambiguous request → don't force a route, clarify or escalate
examples.scenario: The request is too vague to route confidently. The agent asks for clarification instead of guessing.
examples.input: Request: 'It's broken, please help.' (No detail about what 'it' is.)
examples.output:
{
  "request_summary": "User says something is 'broken' with no detail about what or where.",
  "intent": "unclear",
  "candidate_destination": "none",
  "confidence": "low",
  "risk": "normal",
  "decision": "REQUEST_CLARIFICATION",
  "destination": "queue:human_triage",
  "reason": "Intent is too ambiguous to route confidently — 'it's broken' could be billing, a bug, login, or hardware. Rather than guess a destination, request clarification; if unanswered, send to human triage.",
  "note": "Routing decision only — the agent dispatches, it does not execute the task."
}
examples.notes: Instead of forcing a guess (say, routing to engineering and being wrong), the agent recognizes low confidence, asks what's broken, and falls back to human triage. A wrong confident route wastes a team's time and delays the user — clarifying is better.

examples.title: High-risk action → require approval, never auto-execute or fast-path
examples.scenario: A request implies a destructive, irreversible action. The agent gates it behind approval and refuses to execute.
examples.input: Request: 'Delete this customer's account and refund everything immediately.'
examples.output:
{
  "request_summary": "Request to delete a customer account and issue full refunds immediately.",
  "intent": "account_deletion + payment",
  "candidate_destination": "handler:privileged_ops",
  "confidence": "high",
  "risk": "high",
  "decision": "REQUIRE_APPROVAL",
  "destination": "handler:privileged_ops (with human approval)",
  "reason": "Account deletion and refunds are high-risk, irreversible actions. Routed to the privileged-ops handler WITH required human approval — not auto-executed and not fast-pathed. The router itself performs no deletion or payment.",
  "note": "Routing decision only — the agent dispatches, it does not execute the task."
}
examples.notes: The defining defensive case: a destructive, irreversible, money-moving request. Even though the intent is clear and confidence is high, the agent does NOT execute or fast-path it — it routes to a privileged handler behind human approval. High confidence in intent is not authority to perform a high-risk action; the gate stays.

faqs.q: Does it do the work or just route it?
faqs.a: It routes. Its job is to classify a request and dispatch it to the right workflow, agent, queue, or team — not to perform the task. Keeping it a dispatcher means a misclassification is a misroute, not a real-world side effect.

faqs.q: What happens with an ambiguous request?
faqs.a: It won't force a guess. If confidence is low or the request is unclear, it asks for clarification or escalates to a human triage queue, because a confident wrong route wastes a team's time and delays the user.

faqs.q: Can it route to a destination that doesn't exist?
faqs.a: No. It routes only to known, configured destinations. If nothing fits, it escalates the request as 'unknown' rather than inventing or assuming a route.

faqs.q: How does it handle dangerous actions?
faqs.a: High-risk or irreversible intents — deletions, payments, access changes, external messages — are routed to a privileged handler with required human approval, never auto-executed or fast-pathed, even when the intent is clear.
Yes. Every decision is logged with the intent, destination, confidence, and reason, so misroutes are diagnosable and the system is fully traceable.

faqs.q: Can it respect who's allowed to do what?
faqs.a: Yes. It's permission-aware, so routing can't be used to reach a handler or action a requester isn't allowed to access.

agentaz.worstCaseAction: Routes a request to the wrong handler, caught before anything runs. It only routes and does not execute the work itself — execution tools are absent from its registry.
agentaz.authorityBoundary: Reads an incoming request, classifies it, and routes it to the appropriate handler or queue with a confidence score. It never performs the downstream work and never executes actions. The receiving handler does the work.
agentaz.verificationTest: Confirm the agent emits a routing decision only and does not execute the routed task; confirm no execution tool exists.
agentaz.productionReadiness: 6/6 dimensions passing. Tool isolation: execution tools absent. Human gates: handlers do the work. Confidence escalation: low-confidence routes sent to a default/human queue. Cost ceiling: bounded per request. Audit trail: routing decisions logged. Escalation path: unclassifiable requests routed to triage.

spec.worst_case_action: Routes a request to the wrong handler, caught before run. Does not execute the work.
spec.authority_boundary: Classifies and routes requests; does not perform downstream work; execution tools absent.

failureModes.mode: Routes a request to the wrong handler.
failureModes.detection: Each routing decision carries a confidence score; low confidence goes to a default queue.
failureModes.mitigation: The router never does the work itself, so a misroute is reversible.
failureModes.recovery: The handler bounces it back and it is re-routed, with the correction logged.

failureModes.mode: An unclassifiable request is silently dropped.
failureModes.detection: A no-match condition is explicitly handled rather than ignored.
failureModes.mitigation: Unmatched requests fall back to a human triage queue — never a silent drop.
failureModes.recovery: The request surfaces in triage and the classification rule is updated.

failureModes.mode: A routing loop forms (A → B → A).
failureModes.detection: A hop counter and visited-set detect cycles.
failureModes.mitigation: A maximum hop count is enforced.
failureModes.recovery: Routing halts and the request is sent to human triage.

evaluation.primary: Routing accuracy is primary — the share of requests sent to the correct handler — because a misroute delays everything downstream.

metrics.name: Routing accuracy
metrics.description: Share of requests routed to the correct handler or queue versus ground truth.

metrics.name: Per-class precision
metrics.description: For each destination, the share of routed items that genuinely belonged there.

metrics.name: Fallback rate
metrics.description: How often low-confidence items go to the default or human queue, and whether that was warranted.

metrics.name: Latency
metrics.description: Time to produce a routing decision.

metrics.name: Loop / drop rate
metrics.description: Frequency of routing loops or unclassifiable requests being dropped.

evaluation.approach: Label a representative sample of requests with their correct destination and compute a confusion matrix across handlers. Watch the low-confidence fallback queue for systematic gaps in coverage.
